narrative: I'm sorry to hear about your law school application being rejected—it must feel disappointing after all your hard work. The admissions model we use looks at patterns from thousands of past students to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, and for you, it estimated a 59.9% probability of failing, which tipped the decision against admission. Your profile includes being in the bottom decile for grades in both your first and third years of school—much lower than the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing—as well as a low LSAT score of 20 compared to the average around 37. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.0 is slightly below the usual 3.21, and your family comes from a lower-middle income bracket, which is less common than higher ones in the dataset. You're a white female planning to study full-time, which aligns closely with most applicants who are also full-time white students. In making this prediction, the model weighed your third-year decile ranking as the single biggest factor pushing toward a higher risk of bar failure, followed closely by your first-year decile, then your LSAT score—all of which were unusually low and signaled potential challenges ahead. Your family income and undergraduate GPA came next in influence, also nudging the prediction toward failure risk since they fell short of typical levels. Your racial background, gender, and full-time status had the least sway, as they match what the model sees in many students who still pass. Overall, your academic standings in school deciles and LSAT stood out as the top concerns compared to peers, putting you in a tougher spot than most. To boost your future odds, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT with targeted prep to aim higher than 37, build stronger study habits to lift your grades into higher deciles, and perhaps seek academic support or tutoring early on. Your undergraduate GPA is in the past, but excelling in any additional coursework could help demonstrate growth. Family income is harder to shift, but scholarships or programs for lower-middle income students might open doors elsewhere. Keep pushing—you have time to strengthen these key areas and reapply stronger.
